microscopic_findings.text: Tumor shows mixed **cribriform** and **tubular** nests/cords with focal solid areas in a hyalinized stroma.
Numerous **punched-out pseudocysts** contain dense eosinophilic **hyaline basement membrane-like material**.
Cells are monomorphic **basaloid** cells with **hyperchromatic angulated nuclei** and scant cytoplasm.
There is no convincing **keratinization**, **surface dysplasia**, or mucocyte-rich/mucinous differentiation.

interpretation: The combination of **adenoid cystic carcinoma** morphology, including mixed **cribriform/tubular architecture** and hyaline pseudocystic spaces, supports a salivary gland-type tracheal neoplasm. The uniform basaloid cytology and basement membrane-like material are classic for **ACC**. **Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma** is disfavored by the lack of surface dysplasia or keratinization, and **mucoepidermoid carcinoma** is disfavored by the absence of mucocytes or mucinous cell populations.

tiles.description:
The tumor is composed of nests and cords of cells with clear to vacuolated cytoplasm and small, hyperchromatic nuclei, separated by a prominent fibrous stroma. The growth pattern is infiltrative, extending between tracheal rings.

The most likely diagnosis is a clear cell carcinoma of the trachea. Differential diagnoses include metastatic clear cell carcinoma (e.g., renal cell carcinoma) and other clear cell neoplasms such as mucoepidermoid carcinoma or adenoid cystic carcinoma.
tiles.rationale: Whole-region overview to orient and locate cartilage, mucosa, tumor bulk.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with prominent cribriform spaces containing eosinophilic material. The cells are small with scant cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma and small cell carcinoma. Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma typically shows peripheral palisading and squamous differentiation, while small cell carcinoma is characterized by nuclear molding, crush artifact, and a high mitotic rate.
tiles.rationale: central overview to identify cellular tumor areas vs stroma/necrosis

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a mixed growth pattern including solid nests, cords, and cribriform structures with punched-out spaces. The cells are relatively uniform with scant cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows more prominent peripheral palisading and squamous differentiation, and small cell carcinoma, which is characterized by small cells with scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding, and a high mitotic rate.
tiles.rationale: Survey left/upper portion for tumor architecture and borders.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation with a prominent cribriform pattern and nests of cells within a fibrous stroma. The cells have small, hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, with some lumens containing eosinophilic material.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic cribriform architecture, nests of basaloid cells, and the presence of hyaline material within the pseudolumens. The clinical history of a tracheal mass causing obstruction is also consistent with this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses include basal cell carcinoma and other salivary gland-type tumors. Basal cell carcinoma typically lacks the prominent cribriform pattern and hyaline material seen here. Other salivary gland-type tumors may have overlapping features but often show different architectural patterns or cellular morphology.
tiles.rationale: Central low-power survey to localize gland-like/cribriform areas and stroma

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a prominent cribriform pattern and nests of cells with small, hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm. The stroma is hyalinized and contains scattered cystic spaces.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma and small cell carcinoma, which can be distinguished by their different architectural patterns and cytologic features.
tiles.rationale: upper-left cellular area on thumbnail; look for cribriform/gland-like spaces

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent cribriform pattern containing eosinophilic material. The cells are small with scant cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei, set within a fibrous stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows peripheral palisading and squamous differentiation, and small cell carcinoma, which is characterized by nuclear molding, crush artifact, and a high mitotic rate.
tiles.rationale: upper-right area where small lumina/holes seen on thumbnail

tiles.description:
The tumor is composed of nests and cords of basaloid cells with peripheral palisading and central areas showing cribriform and cystic patterns. The stroma is fibrous and hyalinized.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basal cell carcinoma and basaloid squamous cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma typically lacks the prominent cribriform and cystic patterns seen here. Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma would show more overt squamous differentiation and often has a more infiltrative growth pattern.
tiles.rationale: Left-upper portion survey for different architecture

tiles.description:
The image shows a neoplasm with a mixed pattern of tubular and solid areas, composed of cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round to oval nuclei, infiltrating the tracheal wall. The presence of a mixed pattern and infiltrative growth is notable.

The most likely diagnosis is a low-grade mucoepidermoid carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the mixed tubular and solid growth pattern and the infiltrative nature of the tumor.

Differential diagnoses include adenoid cystic carcinoma, which typically shows a cribriform pattern and perineural invasion, and squamous cell carcinoma, which would show squamous differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Survey right/upper portion; possible mucosa/cartilage nearby on thumbnail.

tiles.description: The image shows a neoplasm with a mixed growth pattern including tubular, cribriform, and solid areas, composed of small cells with scant cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei within a fibrous stroma.
tiles.rationale: lower-central more eosinophilic zone; assess invasion/necrosis/stroma

tiles.description:
The tumor is composed of nests and cords of cells with abundant clear to vacuolated cytoplasm, separated by a prominent fibrous stroma. The cells have round to oval nuclei with visible nucleoli.

The most likely diagnosis is a clear cell carcinoma of the trachea.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic renal cell carcinoma, which can have a similar clear cell morphology, and mucoepidermoid carcinoma, which can have clear cell features but typically shows a mixture of cell types.
tiles.rationale: Survey left/lower portion for interface with deeper wall.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation with a prominent cribriform pattern and numerous small cystic spaces. The cells are relatively uniform with round to oval nuclei and scant cytoplasm, embedded in a fibrous stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic cribriform architecture and the presence of small cystic spaces within the tumor. The uniform cellular morphology is also consistent with this entity.

Differential diagnoses to consider include other salivary gland-type tumors that can occur in the trachea, such as pleomorphic adenoma or basal cell adenoma. However, the prominent cribriform pattern and the presence of cystic spaces are more characteristic of adenoid cystic carcinoma.
tiles.rationale: Right-upper portion survey; possible submucosa/ductal areas

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a prominent fibrous capsule and a mixed growth pattern including solid areas and tubular/glandular structures. The tumor cells are relatively uniform with round to oval nuclei and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is a low-grade adenocarcinoma of the trachea. Differential diagnoses include a low-grade neuroendocrine tumor, which can also present as a submucosal mass with a mixed growth pattern, and a low-grade mucoepidermoid carcinoma, which typically shows a mixture of mucous, intermediate, and epidermoid cells.
tiles.rationale: Survey right/lower portion for cartilage/peripheral tissue.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a prominent cribriform pattern and nests of cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, separated by fibrous stroma. The tumor infiltrates the tracheal wall and is associated with a desmoplastic reaction.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows more prominent peripheral palisading and squamous differentiation, and small cell carcinoma, which would have a different cytologic appearance with scant cytoplasm and nuclear molding.
tiles.rationale: Right-lower portion survey near edge/cartilage if present

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent cribriform pattern. The cells are small with scant cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei, separated by a fibrous stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic cribriform architecture, small basaloid cells, and hyaline material within the pseudocysts. Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows more overt squamous differentiation and keratinization, and small cell carcinoma, which usually exhibits a more diffuse growth pattern and nuclear molding.
tiles.rationale: high power cytology in cribriform basaloid area

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent cribriform pattern containing eosinophilic hyaline material. The cells are small with hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, set within a fibrous stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows more prominent peripheral palisading and squamous differentiation, and small cell carcinoma, which is characterized by a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, nuclear molding, and a more diffuse growth pattern.
tiles.rationale: high power cytology in another cribriform focus; look for hyaline globules

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a prominent cribriform pattern and nests of cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, infiltrating the tracheal wall. The tumor is associated with a desmoplastic stromal reaction and areas of necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows more prominent peripheral palisading and squamous differentiation, and small cell carcinoma, which would have a different cytologic appearance with scant cytoplasm and nuclear molding.
tiles.rationale: Right mid-periphery to assess relationship to tracheal wall and any cartilage.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords within a fibrous stroma. The cells have round to oval nuclei and scant cytoplasm, with some areas showing small lumens or pseudoglandular spaces.

The most likely diagnosis is a low-grade neuroendocrine tumor, such as a carcinoid tumor. The nested and cord-like architecture, uniform cells with round nuclei, and presence of pseudoglandular spaces are characteristic features. Differential diagnoses include other neuroendocrine tumors like atypical carcinoid or small cell carcinoma, which would typically show more nuclear pleomorphism, mitotic activity, or necrosis, respectively.
tiles.rationale: high power in mixed tubular/solid area; assess mitoses/necrosis

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent cribriform pattern containing eosinophilic hyaline material. The cells are small with scant cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows peripheral palisading and more overt squamous differentiation, and small cell carcinoma, which is characterized by nuclear molding, crush artifact, and a different growth pattern.
tiles.rationale: 10x at central cribriform focus to confirm architecture and stromal hyalinization

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a prominent cribriform pattern and nests of cells within a fibrous stroma. The cells have small, hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows more prominent peripheral palisading and squamous differentiation, and small cell carcinoma, which would have a different nuclear morphology and growth pattern.
tiles.rationale: Higher power architecture at right lower-mid where stroma/cartilage suspected.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with prominent cribriform spaces containing eosinophilic material. The cells are small with scant cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei, set within a fibrous stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic cribriform pattern, small basaloid cells, and hyaline material within the pseudocysts. Differential diagnoses include basal cell carcinoma and polymorphous adenocarcinoma, which can share some overlapping features but typically lack the prominent cribriform architecture and hyaline material seen here.
tiles.rationale: 10x at right-upper cribriform/microcystic area to assess for true glands vs pseudocysts

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent cribriform pattern. The cells are small with hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, and there are cystic spaces containing eosinophilic material.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic cribriform architecture, small basaloid cells, and the presence of cystic spaces containing eosinophilic material. The clinical history of a tracheal mass causing obstruction is also consistent with this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses to consider include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma and small cell carcinoma. Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma typically shows more prominent squamous differentiation and keratinization, which is not evident here. Small cell carcinoma would show more nuclear molding, crush artifact, and a higher mitotic rate, and would lack the prominent cribriform pattern and cystic spaces seen in this image.
tiles.rationale: high power in central area to assess for NE chromatin, squamous differentiation, mucin cells

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of small cells with hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, arranged in nests and cords within a fibrous stroma. The tumor infiltrates the tracheal wall and extends beyond the cartilage.

The most likely diagnosis is small cell carcinoma of the lung. The differential diagnosis includes other neuroendocrine tumors such as carcinoid tumor, which typically shows more uniform cells and organoid nesting, and lymphoma, which would have a different immunophenotype.
tiles.rationale: Lower right to look for capsule, invasion into cartilage/peritracheal tissue.

tiles.description:
The tumor is composed of nests and cords of cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, separated by prominent fibrous stroma. The growth pattern is infiltrative, extending between tracheal rings.

The most likely diagnosis is a low-grade neuroendocrine tumor, such as a carcinoid tumor. The differential diagnosis includes other neuroendocrine neoplasms like atypical carcinoid or small cell carcinoma, which would show higher mitotic activity and necrosis, respectively.
tiles.rationale: Inferior aspect to see mucosal surface vs deep margin and capsule.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent cribriform pattern. The cells are small with scant cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei, and there are scattered cystic spaces containing eosinophilic material.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic cribriform architecture, small basaloid cells, and the presence of cystic spaces containing eosinophilic material. The clinical history of a tracheal mass causing obstruction is also consistent with this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma and small cell carcinoma. Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma typically shows more prominent squamous differentiation and keratinization, which is not evident here. Small cell carcinoma would show more nuclear molding, crush artifact, and a higher mitotic rate, and would lack the prominent cribriform pattern.
tiles.rationale: 10x at left-upper nests/cords with palisading to exclude basaloid SCC and look for tubular pattern

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with prominent cribriform spaces containing eosinophilic material. The cells are small with hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, set within a fibrous stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows more prominent peripheral palisading and squamous differentiation, and small cell carcinoma, which is characterized by nuclear molding, crush artifact, and a different growth pattern.
tiles.rationale: sample additional superficial tumor for any squamous differentiation or mucocytes

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent cribriform pattern. The cells are small with hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, and the stroma is fibrous.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma and small cell carcinoma, which can be distinguished by their different architectural patterns and cytologic features.
tiles.rationale: 10x at right-lower infiltrative edge; look for perineural invasion or cartilage involvement

tiles.description:
The image shows a neoplasm with a mixed growth pattern including solid nests, cords, and prominent cribriform areas with eosinophilic hyaline material. The cells are relatively uniform with scant cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows more overt squamous differentiation and keratinization, and small cell carcinoma, which is characterized by small cells with scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding, and a high mitotic rate.
tiles.rationale: Central tumor at 5x to confirm dominant pattern (cribriform/tubular/solid) and stromal hyalinization.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent cribriform pattern. The cells are small with hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, and the spaces contain eosinophilic material.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma and small cell carcinoma. Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma typically shows peripheral palisading and squamous differentiation, while small cell carcinoma is characterized by nuclear molding, crush artifact, and a high mitotic rate.
tiles.rationale: edge/superior right focus to look for surface epithelium connection/papilloma-like changes

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords within a fibrous stroma. The cells have clear to vacuolated cytoplasm and small, round nuclei, with some nests containing central lumens.

The most likely diagnosis is a clear cell neoplasm, such as a clear cell carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic renal cell carcinoma, which can have a similar clear cell morphology, and other clear cell tumors like clear cell adenocarcinoma or clear cell sarcoma.
tiles.rationale: deep/lower area for necrosis, mitoses, stromal change (myxoid/chondroid)

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a prominent fibrous capsule and a dense cellular infiltrate. The cells are small with hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, arranged in sheets and nests.

The most likely diagnosis is a small cell carcinoma of the lung.

Differential diagnoses include lymphoma, which can also present as a cellular neoplasm with small cells, and other neuroendocrine tumors like carcinoid tumor, which typically have more abundant cytoplasm and less nuclear hyperchromasia.
tiles.rationale: Far right-lower edge where cartilage/margin likely; assess invasion and capsule.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords within a fibrous stroma. The cells have small, hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, with some nests containing central lumens or pseudolumens.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic cribriform and tubular growth patterns, the presence of small basaloid cells, and the presence of pseudolumens containing hyaline material. The clinical history of a tracheal mass causing obstruction is also consistent with this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma and small cell carcinoma. Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma typically shows more prominent squamous differentiation and keratinization, which is not evident here. Small cell carcinoma would show more nuclear molding, crush artifact, and a higher mitotic rate, which are not prominent features in this image.
tiles.rationale: Highest-yield cribriform focus; assess hyaline globules/BM material, cytology, mitoses

tiles.description:
The image shows a tumor infiltrating the tracheal wall with a prominent desmoplastic stromal reaction. The tumor cells are arranged in nests and cords, with some areas showing a cribriform pattern. The tumor extends between the tracheal rings and is associated with a connective tissue capsule.

The most likely diagnosis is a low-grade adenocarcinoma, such as adenoid cystic carcinoma, given the cribriform pattern and infiltrative growth. A differential diagnosis could include a low-grade mucoepidermoid carcinoma, which can also show infiltrative growth and a desmoplastic stroma, but typically has a mixture of mucous, intermediate, and epidermoid cells.
tiles.rationale: Right lateral projection (polypoid?) to see surface/mucosa and growth pattern.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent cribriform pattern. The cells have small, hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, with eosinophilic material within the pseudolumina.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma and small cell carcinoma. Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma typically shows peripheral palisading and squamous differentiation, while small cell carcinoma is characterized by nuclear molding, crush artifact, and a high mitotic rate.
tiles.rationale: Cribriform/microcystic area; look for true ducts, mucin cells (mucoepidermoid) vs pseudocysts

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a prominent vascular pattern and areas of hemorrhage and necrosis. The tumor cells are arranged in nests and cords within a fibrous stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is a neuroendocrine tumor, such as a carcinoid tumor, given the cellular morphology and vascular pattern. Differential diagnoses include other neuroendocrine neoplasms like small cell carcinoma or large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma, which would typically show more pleomorphism and mitotic activity.
tiles.rationale: Inferior midline deeper tissue to identify cartilage ring and extraluminal extension.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent cribriform pattern. The cells are small with hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, separated by a delicate fibrovascular stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic cribriform architecture, small basaloid cells, and hyaline material within the pseudolumina. Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows more overt squamous differentiation and keratinization, and small cell carcinoma, which usually exhibits a more diffuse growth pattern and nuclear molding.
tiles.rationale: Peripheral palisading area; exclude basaloid SCC (keratinization/intercellular bridges) and look for dual cell population

tiles.description:
The tumor is composed of nests and cords of cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, embedded in a prominent fibrous stroma. The tumor infiltrates the tracheal wall and extends between cartilaginous rings.

The most likely diagnosis is a neuroendocrine tumor, such as a carcinoid tumor, given the nested and cord-like architecture and the presence of a fibrous stroma. Differential diagnoses include other neuroendocrine neoplasms like atypical carcinoid or small cell carcinoma, which would show different cytologic features and growth patterns.
tiles.rationale: Bottom edge to assess inked margin/capsule and relationship to surrounding soft tissue.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords within a fibrous stroma. The cells have small, hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, with some nests containing central lumens or cystic spaces.

The most likely diagnosis is a low-grade salivary gland-type neoplasm, such as adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include other salivary gland-type tumors like pleomorphic adenoma or basal cell adenoma, which can have similar architectural patterns but typically show more prominent stromal components or different cellular morphology.
tiles.rationale: Infiltrative edge; look for perineural invasion and stromal reaction

tiles.description:
The image shows a large, irregular tumor with a central area of necrosis and hemorrhage, surrounded by a dense cellular infiltrate. The tumor extends between tracheal rings and is associated with a connective tissue capsule.

The most likely diagnosis is a malignant neoplasm, likely a carcinoma, given the location in the trachea and the patient's history of progressive airway obstruction and weight loss. Differential diagnoses include squamous cell carcinoma and adenocarcinoma, which are common primary tracheal tumors. Other possibilities include metastatic carcinoma or a sarcoma, although these are less likely given the clinical presentation.
tiles.rationale: Left inferior small detached fragment to determine if tumor present or benign tissue.

tiles.description:
The image shows a neoplasm with a prominent cribriform pattern and tubular structures within a fibrous stroma. The cells are relatively uniform with round to oval nuclei and moderate cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic cribriform and tubular growth patterns, the presence of a fibrous stroma, and the relatively uniform cellular morphology. The clinical history of a tracheal mass causing obstruction and recurrence is also consistent with this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses to consider include other salivary gland-type tumors such as pleomorphic adenoma or basal cell adenoma, which can also occur in the trachea. However, these typically lack the prominent cribriform pattern and infiltrative growth seen in adenoid cystic carcinoma.
tiles.rationale: Top-right to look for tracheal mucosa/surface and polypoid luminal component.

tiles.description:
The image shows a neoplasm with a mixed growth pattern including tubular, cribriform, and solid areas. The tumor cells are relatively uniform with round to oval nuclei and scant cytoplasm, and there is a prominent hyalinized stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic mixed architectural patterns (tubular, cribriform, solid) and the presence of hyalinized stroma. The uniform cytology with round to oval nuclei and scant cytoplasm is also typical. Differential diagnoses include other salivary gland-type tumors such as pleomorphic adenoma, which typically shows a more prominent chondromyxoid stroma and a mixture of epithelial and myoepithelial cells, and basal cell adenoma, which lacks the cribriform pattern and hyalinized stroma.
tiles.rationale: Explore far right edge near lumen to look for surface epithelium/papilloma/squamous dysplasia or cartilage involvement

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent cribriform pattern. The cells have small, hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, with hyaline material within the pseudolumina.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows more prominent peripheral palisading and squamous differentiation, and small cell carcinoma, which would have a different nuclear morphology and growth pattern.
tiles.rationale: Inferior portion; look for chondromyxoid stroma or pleomorphic adenoma-like areas

tiles.description:
The image shows a low-power view of a tracheal wall with a central irregular tumor. The tumor appears to grow between the tracheal rings and is associated with a connective tissue capsule. The tumor is fairly soft and cut easily, with a homogeneous dull gray moist cut surface.

The most likely diagnosis is a tracheal neoplasm, likely a squamous cell carcinoma given the clinical history of progressive airway obstruction and the gross appearance of a large, irregular tumor. Differential diagnoses include other primary tracheal tumors such as adenocarcinoma or neuroendocrine tumors, or metastatic disease to the trachea.
tiles.rationale: Bottom-right edge to confirm capsule/margin and any cartilage.

tiles.description:
The tumor is composed of nests and cords of basaloid cells with peripheral palisading and central cystic spaces, set within a fibrous stroma. The overlying surface epithelium is intact.

The most likely diagnosis is basaloid carcinoma of the trachea.

Differential diagnoses include adenoid cystic carcinoma, which typically shows cribriform architecture and hyaline stroma, and squamous cell carcinoma, which would exhibit squamous differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Top-left edge for mucosa and tumor border.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent cribriform pattern containing eosinophilic material. The cells are small with hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, set within a fibrous stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic cribriform architecture, small basaloid cells, and the presence of eosinophilic hyaline material within the pseudocysts. The clinical presentation of a tracheal mass causing obstruction is also consistent with this tumor type.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which can also show basaloid cells and a cribriform pattern, but typically lacks the prominent hyaline material and often shows areas of squamous differentiation. Polymorphous adenocarcinoma is another consideration, but it usually exhibits a more varied architectural pattern and lacks the classic cribriform morphology seen here.
tiles.rationale: Mid-lower area; search for perineural invasion or tubular pattern variation

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a prominent cribriform pattern and scattered cystic spaces. The tumor cells are relatively uniform with scant cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei, arranged in nests and cords.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic cribriform architecture, uniform cell population, and presence of cystic spaces. Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which can also show a cribriform pattern but typically has more prominent squamous differentiation and keratinization, and small cell carcinoma, which would show a different cellular morphology with scant cytoplasm and nuclear molding.
tiles.rationale: Left mid to assess cribriform vs solid areas and stromal hyalinization.

tiles.description:
The image shows a neoplasm with a predominant cribriform pattern composed of nests and cords of cells with small, hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, forming pseudolumens containing eosinophilic material. A desmoplastic stroma is also present.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, which typically shows more prominent peripheral palisading and squamous differentiation, and small cell carcinoma, which usually exhibits a more diffuse growth pattern and nuclear molding.
tiles.rationale: Random different focus at higher power to check for mucin cells/epidermoid cells or neuroendocrine chromatin

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a mixed growth pattern including nests, cords, and tubular structures within a prominent hyalinized stroma. The cells are relatively uniform with round to oval nuclei and scant cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is adenoid cystic carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the characteristic cribriform, tubular, and solid growth patterns, the presence of hyalinized stroma, and the relatively uniform cell population. The clinical history of a tracheal mass causing significant obstruction is also consistent with this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses to consider include basaloid squamous cell carcinoma and small cell carcinoma. Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma typically shows more prominent peripheral palisading and often has areas of squamous differentiation. Small cell carcinoma is characterized by smaller cells with scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding, and a high mitotic rate, which are not prominent features in this image.
tiles.rationale: Cribriform area at higher power to assess pseudocysts/hyaline material and any perineural invasion structures.
